trajectory_summary: A compressed recycled-lead callback that moves from guarded to cooperative in under two minutes. The design-framing opener was the right strategic call for this context, and the customer booked without resistance. The appointment is structurally thin: no bill captured, no computer anchor, no verbal handshake, and the co-decision-maker situation (mother was present at the prior appointment) is unresolved.
outcome_dispute: v1 tag is 'unknown' — this call ends with a booked appointment for Friday 05:30. Outcome should be tagged 'appointment_booked' with a 'conditional' flag: bill not captured, button-up incomplete, prior-contact decision-maker (mother) not addressed.

primary_coaching_focus.why: The customer confirmed he'd be home and awake — the lowest-friction window in the call — and Meg did not insert the computer anchor or verbal handshake. Both are codex-required and both predict show rate. This is a mechanical habit gap, not a judgment call. The rep had the moment and didn't use it.

key_moments.quote: We built a solar design for your home. And on my end, it says we actually never had the chance to show it to you or get you the details.
key_moments.stewart_read: Strategic read holds. On a recycled callback, the design-framing opener is the correct move — it repositions the appointment as completing something already in progress rather than initiating a new sales pitch. This is the moment that converts the customer's 'From where?' challenge into a scheduling conversation. The rep is not over-explaining solar; she's using the existing design as a legitimacy anchor.

key_moments.quote: I I I don't I wasn't there for that appointment. My mom must have been there. I'm not a 100% sure.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is a co-decision-maker disclosure, not a spouse-handling moment. The customer reveals that his mother was the prior contact — she may be on the title, may be the primary decision-maker, and is not confirmed for the Friday appointment. Meg acknowledges the uncertainty ('Gotcha') and moves to bill logistics without addressing whether the mother needs to be present. The codex is explicit: co-decision-makers must be included in the appointment. Secondary reference: protocols.spouse_decision applies by analogy.

key_moments.quote: From ION Solar. Are you returning a missed call?
key_moments.stewart_read: On a self-initiated callback, the legitimacy-establishment burden is materially lower than on a cold outbound call. The customer called ION back — he already accepted the premise of the contact. Meg's response is functional but bare: no Tesla framing, no state mention, no design reference until 00:22. The codex defines intro variants for tesla and standard leads but has no recycled-lead intro variant. This is a codex gap, not a standard-intro violation. The callback context makes the abbreviated intro defensible; the absence of a defined recycled-lead intro template is the real flag.

key_moments.quote: Oh, 05:30 is better. Because I work nights, so I'll probably be asleep most of the day.
key_moments.stewart_read: The customer discloses a scheduling constraint (night-shift worker, asleep during the day) that makes the 05:30 slot non-negotiable and the appointment time-sensitive. This is a cooperation signal — he's explaining himself — but it also means any reschedule attempt will face real friction. Meg acknowledges it ('Makes sense. Okay. Gotcha.') but does not anchor the appointment with the button-up elements the codex requires: no computer anchor, no 'not driving' check, no verbal handshake.
